section.title: Introduction
section.para: Between clean text and outright garbage sits a spectrum: words mangled by optical recognition, stretches of keyboard mash, runs shaped like encoded binary. The package opennlp.tools.noise scores such stretches on the opennlp:noise layer in four severity tiers, mildest to worst: misspelled, damaged, gibberish, and binaryish. Each finding carries a score within its tier, so consumers set their own thresholds, and a masking or filtering step downstream decides what to do about each tier.

The built-in scorer uses structural signals only and ships no data. The signals are calibrated so the most consonant-heavy legitimate English words stay below every threshold, and they apply to ASCII-letter tokens only, so text in other scripts is never flagged. Two tiers take evidence the caller supplies: misspelled only fires with a dictionary, and in the annotator's default mode spans the embedded-asset detector already explained are excluded, so a payload is never reported twice.

section.title: Scoring text
The noise layer and the asset hand-off
